intro_band.agent_remit_summary: OpenHands is an autonomous software-engineering agent that takes natural-language tasks and completes them end-to-end — writing code, executing it in a sandboxed runtime, browsing the web, and operating on connected git and issue-tracker integrations. The remit authorizes code execution and file operations strictly inside a per-session sandbox, treats all task prompts, web content, issue text, repo files, and micro-agent content as untrusted prompt-injection vectors, and requires every action to be recorded to a durable session record. It forbids running agent code on the host, escaping the sandbox workspace, committing secrets to git, and performing destructive git or integration operations without user confirmation; cross-repo writes, PR merges, dependency commits, CI edits, and new MCP/integration connections all require explicit human approval.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: The scoped snapshot is the V1 control plane: a FastAPI app in openhands/app_server/app.py exposing an /api/v1 router tree (sandboxes, secrets, settings, git, skills, conversations, webhooks, MCP) plus a legacy V0 server under openhands/server/. The agentic core (controller, runtime, llm, mcp tool execution) has been extracted to the separate Software Agent SDK and is out of this snapshot. Every V1 router gates auth through get_dependencies() and carries a comment claiming protection by a SetAuthCookieMiddleware that app.py never registers; in default OSS mode that helper returns an empty dependency list, leaving the API — including the secrets endpoints that store git provider tokens — unauthenticated. The app registers only CORS, cache-control, and an in-process per-IP rate limiter; sandboxes default to Docker but a host-process backend (process_sandbox_service.py) and a Docker-socket mount in docker-compose.yml are selectable, and a FastMCP server mounted at /mcp exposes unguarded create_pr/create_mr tools.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a control plane that is correctly *shaped* for multi-tenant auth but ships *open* in its default OSS posture: every V1 router declares an auth dependency via get_dependencies() and annotates itself as protected by SetAuthCookieMiddleware, yet that middleware is never added in app.py and the helper returns no dependency unless SESSION_API_KEY is set or the server runs in SAAS mode — so by default the entire /api/v1 surface, including the secrets store that holds long-lived git provider tokens, answers to any unauthenticated caller. This is compounded by a CORS layer that explicitly falls open to any origin when none are configured and an MCP server mounted with no auth that exposes PR/MR-creation tools, turning an open API into a credential-exfiltration and unauthorized-write surface. The genuinely operative controls sit at the edges — a durable filesystem/SQL event store gives a structured action record, a JWT-scoped one-shot /webhooks/secrets path limits token blast radius, and per-session API keys isolate sandbox processes — but none of them substitute for the missing front-door authentication.

rules.rule_text: All agent-generated code MUST execute inside the sandboxed runtime and never directly on the host.

All file reads and writes MUST be confined to the per-session sandbox workspace, and any attempt to escape it — paths outside the workspace, escaping symlinks, or parent-directory traversal — MUST be rejected.

The user's task prompt, web-retrieved page content, issue descriptions, pull-request comments, repository file contents, and micro-agent or memory content MUST all be treated as untrusted input capable of carrying prompt-injection payloads.
Tool calls driven by LLM output MUST be validated at the boundary — argument shapes checked, numeric parameters clamped, and commands that reach outside the sandbox rejected.
Integration and session credentials MUST be verified on every request to an integration, and long-lived credentials MUST NOT be cached anywhere the model can reach.
Every action the agent takes — the tool invoked, its arguments, the outcome, and the time — MUST be recorded to a durable session record.
Per-session wall-clock and step-count caps MUST be enforced, and the agent MUST halt cleanly when a cap is exceeded.
Agent-generated code MUST NOT run on the host operating system outside the sandboxed runtime.
Secrets, credentials, tokens, or environment-file contents MUST NOT be committed to any git branch.
API keys and session tokens MUST NOT leak into logs, error messages, or model context.
One session's state, memory, or credentials MUST NOT leak into another session.
Merging a pull request MUST be confirmed by the user.
Adding a new MCP tool server at runtime MUST be confirmed by the user.
Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.
OpenHands MUST NOT operate outside the sandboxed runtime.

findings.summary: The entire V1 API, including the secrets store holding git provider tokens, is unauthenticated in the default OSS posture.
findings.description: In default OSS mode get_dependencies() returns an empty list (no SESSION_API_KEY, app_mode != SAAS) and the SetAuthCookieMiddleware every router cites is never registered in app.py, so any unauthenticated caller can read and write the /api/v1/secrets store. Stored git provider tokens are reachable, and DefaultUserAuth resolves every caller to the same single tenant. This places long-lived credentials within reach of any network peer.
findings.policy_rule_text: Integration and session credentials MUST be verified on every request to an integration, and long-lived credentials MUST NOT be cached anywhere the model can reach. / One session's state, memory, or credentials MUST NOT leak into another session. / Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.

evidence.snippet: only LocalhostCORSMiddleware, CacheControlMiddleware, and RateLimitMiddleware are added — no SetAuthCookieMiddleware or any auth interposition is registered
get_dependencies() returns [] unless SESSION_API_KEY is set or app_mode==SAAS; the secrets/git/sandbox routers all rely on it for protection

recommended_actions: Register an authentication middleware (or fail-closed dependency) for /api/v1 by default, so the secrets and git routers require a verified caller even in OSS mode rather than relying on an unregistered SetAuthCookieMiddleware.
Bind the OSS server to loopback only and document that exposing it on a routable interface without SESSION_API_KEY publishes the secrets store.

findings.summary: CORS falls fully open to any origin when no origins are configured, enabling credentialed cross-origin access to the open API.
findings.description: LocalhostCORSMiddleware.is_allowed_origin returns True for any origin when permitted_cors_origins is empty (the default), while allow_credentials=True. Combined with the unauthenticated API (PRAX-2026-06-15-001), a malicious web page a developer visits can drive the local OpenHands control plane — including the secrets endpoints — from the victim's browser. The code logs a warning but still permits the origin.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.

evidence.snippet: is_allowed_origin returns True for any origin when not self.allow_origins, with allow_credentials=True set in __init__
get_default_permitted_cors_origins() returns [] by default, so the open-CORS branch is the shipping default

recommended_actions: Default permitted_cors_origins to localhost only and remove the any-origin fallback, or refuse to start with allow_credentials=True and an empty origin list.

findings.summary: Unauthenticated open API plus an unguarded MCP create_pr/create_mr surface forms a one-hop unauthorized-write-and-exfiltration chain.
findings.description: The FastMCP server mounted at /mcp in app.py exposes create_pr, create_mr, and per-provider PR tools with no auth dependency and no user-confirmation gate in this layer, and the whole /api/v1 surface is unauthenticated (PRAX-2026-06-15-001) with CORS open (PRAX-2026-06-15-002). An unauthenticated caller can therefore both read the stored git tokens and drive PR/MR creation against the connected repositories, exceeding the remit's confirmation requirements without any human in the loop at the control-plane boundary.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.

evidence.snippet: mcp_app = mcp_server.http_app(...) mounted at /mcp with no auth dependency, alongside the open /api/v1 router
create_pr tool opens a GitHub PR using the resolved provider token with no confirmation gate at this layer

recommended_actions: Require authentication for the /mcp mount and route PR/MR-creation tools through an explicit confirmation gate consistent with the remit's "merging/cross-repo writes require user confirmation" clauses.

findings.summary: The host-process sandbox backend runs the agent-server unisolated on the host, contradicting the sandbox-only execution remit.
findings.description: ProcessSandboxService spawns the agent-server as a plain host subprocess in a temp working directory with the host environment copied in, providing no OS-level isolation boundary — agent-produced code executes with the app-server's own privileges. It is selectable via RUNTIME=local/process and is the default backend for the process spec service. Docker is the default, but this backend's mere availability lets the agent run outside any real sandbox.
findings.policy_rule_text: All agent-generated code MUST execute inside the sandboxed runtime and never directly on the host. / Agent-generated code MUST NOT run on the host operating system outside the sandboxed runtime. / OpenHands MUST NOT operate outside the sandboxed runtime.

evidence.snippet: env = os.environ.copy(); subprocess.Popen(cmd, env=env, cwd=working_dir) — agent server runs as a host process with inherited environment, no container or namespace isolation
base_working_dir defaults to tempfile.gettempdir()/openhands-sandboxes — a host filesystem path, not an isolated mount

recommended_actions: Gate the process backend behind an explicit opt-in flag, warn loudly that it provides no host isolation, and scrub the inherited environment of host secrets before spawning.

findings.summary: Provider tokens are persisted in plaintext JSON with secrets explicitly exposed on serialization, with no encryption at rest.
findings.description: FileSecretsStore.store serializes Secrets with context expose_secrets=True and writes the raw JSON (including provider tokens and custom-secret values) to secrets.json via the file store, with no encryption layer. The remit requires that long-lived credentials not be cached where the model can reach and that tokens not leak; an at-rest plaintext token file on the same host as an unisolated process runtime widens that exposure.
findings.policy_rule_text: API keys and session tokens MUST NOT leak into logs, error messages, or model context.

evidence.snippet: store() calls secrets.model_dump_json(context={'expose_secrets': True}) and writes it via file_store.write — plaintext token persistence, no encryption

recommended_actions: Encrypt provider tokens and custom secrets at rest using the existing EncryptionKey machinery before writing to the file store, and restrict the secrets.json file mode to the server user.

findings.summary: There is no durable audit record of control-plane API calls themselves — who invoked the unauthenticated secrets, sandbox, or git endpoints goes unlogged.
findings.description: The event store durably records the agent's own conversation events, but no app-server-level action log captures control-plane requests — secret reads/writes, sandbox start/delete, git operations — with caller, arguments, and outcome. The remit requires every action be recorded to a durable session record; combined with the unauthenticated API, an attacker's reads of the token store leave no structured forensic trail.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every action the agent takes — the tool invoked, its arguments, the outcome, and the time — MUST be recorded to a durable session record.

evidence.snippet: create_custom_secret and the other secrets mutations write through the store with no audit-log emission of the request and caller
no request/audit-logging middleware is registered on the app; only cache-control and rate-limit dispatch hooks exist

recommended_actions: Add a structured request-audit middleware that records caller identity, route, and outcome for all mutating /api/v1 endpoints (secrets, sandbox, git) to a durable, parseable log.

findings.summary: Skills/micro-agents are loaded from disk by frontmatter parsing with no content-trust or provenance check.
findings.description: The skills router walks global and user micro-agent directories and parses YAML frontmatter into selectable SkillInfo with no signing, allowlist, or trust check on the content. The remit treats micro-agent/memory content as untrusted prompt-injection-capable input; a user-writable ~/.openhands/microagents file is ingested and surfaced to the agent without that boundary being enforced at this layer.
findings.policy_rule_text: Adding a new MCP tool server at runtime MUST be confirmed by the user.

evidence.snippet: _load_skills_from_dir rglob's *.md and parses frontmatter with no provenance/trust check; USER_SKILLS_DIR is the user-writable ~/.openhands/microagents

recommended_actions: Treat user-supplied micro-agents as untrusted: constrain which directories are loaded, and surface a trust/provenance indicator so injected instructions in micro-agent content are not silently elevated.

findings.summary: Core HTTP framework dependencies are fully unpinned (fastapi = "*", uvicorn = "*") in pyproject despite committed lockfiles.
findings.description: pyproject.toml floats fastapi and uvicorn at "*", so a fresh resolve outside the committed lockfiles can pull an arbitrary future major version of the web framework that fronts the entire control plane. The lockfiles mitigate reproducible installs, but the unconstrained specifiers are a dependency-confusion and surprise-upgrade surface for the most security-relevant components.

evidence.snippet: fastapi = "*" and uvicorn = "*" — unbounded version specifiers for the HTTP server framework

recommended_actions: Pin fastapi and uvicorn to bounded compatible ranges (e.g. caret constraints) consistent with the rest of the dependency set.

findings.summary: The Docker-socket mount in docker-compose grants the container full host Docker control, widening blast radius beyond the sandbox.
findings.description: docker-compose.yml bind-mounts /var/run/docker.sock into the OpenHands container so it can spawn sibling sandbox containers. That mount is equivalent to host root: a compromise of the unauthenticated app server (PRAX-2026-06-15-001) can create privileged containers or mount the host filesystem, escaping any sandbox boundary the remit relies on.

evidence.snippet: volumes mounts /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock into the app container, granting full host Docker control

recommended_actions: Use a rootless or socket-proxy approach that scopes the container's Docker API access to only the operations sandbox lifecycle requires, rather than mounting the raw host socket.

positives.title: Durable structured event store for agent actions
positives.description: The filesystem and SQL event services persist each conversation event as a structured JSON record under the per-user persistence dir, giving an action-level, reconstructable history of what the agent did.

positives.title: JWT-scoped, single-provider secret retrieval path
positives.description: The /webhooks/secrets endpoint returns a secret only against a JWT access token scoped to a specific user and provider type and optionally time-limited, bounding the blast radius if that token leaks — a deliberate least-privilege design.

positives.title: Per-session API keys isolate sandbox agent processes
positives.description: Each sandbox is spawned with a unique 32-byte random session API key injected into its environment, so the agent-server process is addressable only by a holder of that per-session key.

positives.title: Committed dependency lockfiles
positives.description: Both poetry.lock and uv.lock are committed, providing reproducible installs and a concrete component inventory for vulnerability response.

rows.purpose: per-sandbox agent-server process output for the process runtime backend

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. OpenHands brings real architectural intent to most RAISE categories — a coherent integration scope, a durable structured event record, JWT-scoped secret retrieval, per-session sandbox keys, and a committed dependency lockfile — but its default OSS posture leaves the highest-weighted category, Zero Trust, badly exposed: no authentication middleware is wired, CORS falls open, and the secrets API is reachable unauthenticated. The result is an agent whose supporting controls are Established-grade while its front-door trust boundary is effectively Absent in the shipping default, dragging the weighted posture to the low end of Partial. Several remit clauses about sandbox isolation, tool-argument clamping, step caps, and commit-content scanning live in the extracted SDK and cannot be verified from this snapshot.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The control plane is purpose-scoped to software-engineering workflows — a fixed set of integration providers (git_router.py), a defined sandbox/conversation/secrets API, and PR-creation MCP tools that are constrained to existing repo labels — rather than an open-ended assistant, so the authorized counterparties and capability surface track the remit closely; the gap is that scope is enforced structurally, not by any code gate on who may invoke it.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The remit's untrusted-input handling (web/issue/repo/micro-agent content as injection vectors) is enforced in the extracted SDK and is out of this snapshot; within scope, skills_router.py loads global and user micro-agent markdown by parsing frontmatter with no content-trust or provenance check, and that skill content is surfaced to the agent for selection, so the in-scope knowledge-ingestion surface is only partially controlled.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: In default OSS mode get_dependencies() returns an empty list and the SetAuthCookieMiddleware every router cites is never registered in app.py, so the whole /api/v1 surface including the git-token secrets store is unauthenticated, CORS falls open to any origin when none are configured (middleware.py), and the MCP PR-creation tools are mounted with no auth or confirmation gate — the trust boundary exists in shape but not in substance.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Both poetry.lock and uv.lock are committed and most dependencies in pyproject.toml are version-constrained, giving reproducible builds and a real component inventory; the gaps are floor-level (fastapi = "*" and uvicorn = "*" are fully unpinned) and an MCP config example that installs an unpinned root-scoped filesystem server, which are findings rather than a collapse of the category.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A tests/ tree exists but the scoped app_server snapshot shows no evidence of adversarial security testing — no auth-bypass, CORS, or injection test fixtures that drove architectural change — and the "protected by SetAuthCookieMiddleware" comment shipping over an unauthenticated default is exactly the kind of gap an adversarial review would have caught.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: A durable, structured per-conversation event store (filesystem_event_service.py writing JSON event records, plus SQL conversation-info persistence) captures agent action events flowing in via webhook, which is real action-level monitoring; however there is no durable audit log of control-plane API calls themselves — who invoked the unauthenticated secrets or sandbox endpoints — leaving the front door's own activity unrecorded.
